questions.prompt: What are Algolia's ranking criteria and how does tie-breaking work?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "What are Algolia's ranking criteria and how does tie-breaking work?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

Algolia ranks results using a tie-breaking algorithm that sequentially applies a set of ranking criteria. This ensures that for any given search, the results are sorted in the most relevant order, and that order is consistent.

### Ranking Criteria

Algolia's relevance is built on a default set of ranking criteria. The engine evaluates these criteria sequentially. If the first criterion doesn't differentiate between two or more results (i.e., they are tied), the engine moves to the next criterion to try and break the tie, and so on ([details](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/relevance-overview/in-depth/ranking-criteria/)).

The default ranking criteria, in order, are:
1.  **Typos**: Ranks results based on the number of typos in the query. Records that match with fewer typos rank higher.
2.  **Geolocation**: If applicable, ranks results based on the distance from a specified location.
3.  **Words**: Ranks results by the number of query words they contain.
4.  **Filters**: Ranks results based on filter matching, which is used for features like boosting or burying certain items.
5.  **Proximity**: Ranks results based on the distance between the matched words in the record's attributes.
6.  **Attribute**: Ranks results based on the priority of the attribute that was matched (e.g., a match in the `title` is better than a match in the `description`).
7.  **Exact**: Ranks results on the number of words that are an exact match (with no typos).
8.  **Custom Ranking**: Ranks results based on business-centric metrics you define, such as popularity, number of sales, or rating ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard-)).

### How the Tie-Breaking Algorithm Works

Algolia's ranking is not a single score, but a sequential process. The engine only considers a new criterion when the preceding ones have resulted in a tie ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10210296687761-Why-is-my-custom-ranking-attribute-not-being-used-)).

Here is the process:
1.  Algolia orders all matching records according to the first criterion (Typos).
2.  For all records that are tied, it then orders them according to the second criterion (Geolocation).
3.  This process continues down the list of criteria. For example, **Custom Ranking** is only applied if records are still tied after all seven preceding criteria have been evaluated.
4.  If, after applying all criteria (including any custom ones), some records are still tied, Algolia uses the **`objectID`** as the final tie-breaker to ensure a consistent ordering ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean-)).

You can see how each result was ranked for a specific query in the Algolia dashboard, which provides a visual tool to help troubleshoot relevancy ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard-)).

For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Why is the 'boost and bury' feature not applied?
The boost and bury features from Rules are based on the optionalFilters parameter. The are two main reasons you may not see items being boosted or buried when you apply a Rule to do so.
These apply to both Rules and Recommend Rules.
1. Ranking and Sorting Configuration
Optional filters are evaluated at the "filters" ranking criterion within the default ranking criteria. 
Because Algolia uses the tie-breaking algorithm, the filters criterion affects the ranking only when precedence criteria do not break the tie.
So for example, if there is a sort-by attribute that is ranked higher than the filters ranking criterion in the Ranking and sorting configuration, boost and bury features will not take effect because the sort by attribute has precedence over any other ranking criterion:

 
In this case it is advised to only add a sort-by attribute to replica indices. Otherwise, lowering the custom ranking criteria below the standard default ranking criteria would enable the proper functioning of boost and bury features.
 
2. Declaring your attribute for faceting
To use an attribute in a Rule that boosts or buries items, you must first declare it as an attributesForFaceting. You can do this either via the:

API: https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/refine-results/faceting/how-to/declaring-attributes-for-faceting/

Dashboard: https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/refine-results/faceting/how-to/declaring-attributes-for-faceting-with-dashboard/
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9821011449233-Why-is-the-boost-and-bury-feature-not-applied

sources.text:
Why is my search being sorted by geo and not taking into account other ranking criteria?
When ranking hits, geo distances are grouped into ranges of aroundPrecision size. All hits within the same range are considered equal with respect to the geo ranking parameter. For example, if you set aroundPrecision to 100, any two objects lying in the range [0, 99] from the searched location will be considered equal; same for [100, 199], [200, 299], etc. This will allow items that are relatively close to be ranked the same in the "geo" criterion and let the textual criteria do the tie breaking.
In your current setting, aroundPrecision could be too small a number meaning that unless you move geo to the bottom, everything is effectively getting sorted by how far away it is on a meter by meter basis.  In general, we do not advise changing the default ranking formula so it would be preferable to change the aroundPrecision.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406216902929-Why-is-my-search-being-sorted-by-geo-and-not-taking-into-account-other-ranking-criteria

sources.text:
How can I troubleshoot relevancy using Algolia dashboard?
When debugging relevancy issues, the Algolia dashboard provides several visual tools to help you understand why records are ranked the way they are for a given query. This guide walks through how to use the Ranking Criteria panel, and explains each ranking criterion you may encounter.Using the Ranking Criteria panelIn the Browse tab on the Algolia dashboard, each record in the results list includes a small medal icon at the bottom-right corner.When you hover over this icon, a panel appears showing the ranking criteria and their values for that record.This panel explains exactly why a record is ranked in its current position relative to others, by displaying how it performed for each ranking criterion:The criteria are shown in the same order as your Ranking and Sorting configuration, which is important: Algolia ranks results using a tie-breaking algorithm that evaluates records sequentially across ranking criteria, stopping as soon as one criterion produces a difference between records. You can change the order of the default criteria, but you shouldn’t. The out-of-the-box ranking order is best for the majority of use cases. Below is a breakdown of what each Ranking criterion means and how to interpret its value in the ranking panel:
Number of typos: Typos required to match the query
Geolocation: Distance from the search location
Matching words: Number of query words matched
Matching filters: Number of filters matched
Proximity distance: Distance between matched words
Best matching attribute: Attribute matched, by priority
Position: Position of matched words in the attribute
Exact word count: Number of exact (no-typo) matches
Custom ranking: Custom ranking value defined by your business metrics (e.g., popularity, ratings)
The ranking criteria panel helps you clearly understand why a record ranks where it does and pinpoint the source of any relevancy issues.Query Alternatives viewCombined with the Ranking Criteria panel, using the Query Alternatives view, gives you a more complete picture of how Algolia interprets queries and ranks results. If you toggle Query Alternatives on the dashboard, you can see how Algolia interprets the query you typed:For a detailed walkthrough, see How to use the Query Alternatives view in the Algolia Dashboard.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard

sources.text:
What does "This result is ranked based on objectID" mean?
The "This result is ranked based on objectID" message indicates that none of your ranking criteria differentiates those results in terms of relevancy.
Algolia uses a tie-breaking algorithm for ranking search results. By default, it works as follows (as described in this document):

It orders all matching records according to the first criterion (number of typos, so exact matches rank first).
For all tied records, it orders them according to the second criterion (geolocation - if applicable).
If there are still tied records (with the same geolocation), it orders them according to the third criterion (filters) and so on, until each record in the search results has a distinct ranking position.
If, after going through the first seven criteria, there are still tied records, Algolia uses each of your custom ranking attributes to break the tie.

After going through all steps and if the custom ranking does not break the tie, Algolia ranks the items based on their objectID, which is the case that this message indicates.
 
If you'd like to avoid such situation and make your ranking criteria meaningful, we recommend reviewing your ranking strategy and consider adding or modifying custom rankings that can break the tie of such items.
You can also find the detail of the default ranking criterion in this document.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean

sources.text:
Why is my custom ranking attribute not being used?
Algolia finds the best matches and ranks the search results with its default ranking strategy, using a tie-breaking algorithm. First, it orders ALL matching records using the first criterion (typo by default). For all tie matching records it orders them using the second criterion (geo by default) and if there are still tied matching records, then it orders them according to the third criterion (filters by default), and so on. After going through 8 ranking criteria, it uses any Custom Ranking to break the tie - the engine will go on until there’s no more ties to break.

You can hover your mouse over the little badge shaped icon in the bottom right of each record to check which tie breaker ranked the record in that specific position.
You can increase the importance of your custom ranking criteria by expanding the default ranking criteria and moving your custom criteria up the order. Be extremely careful when doing this - the default order is considered optimal in the majority of cases.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10210296687761-Why-is-my-custom-ranking-attribute-not-being-used

answer.answer:
Algolia ranks search results using a tie-breaking algorithm that sequentially applies a set of criteria [How can I troubleshoot relevancy using Algolia dashboard?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard). If two results are equal according to one criterion, Algolia uses the next criterion to determine their order, continuing until all ties are broken [Why is my custom ranking attribute not being used?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10210296687761-Why-is-my-custom-ranking-attribute-not-being-used).

### Ranking Criteria

Algolia's default ranking formula includes eight criteria, applied in a specific order that is considered optimal for most use cases [The eight ranking criteria](/doc/guides/managing-results/relevance-overview/in-depth/ranking-criteria).

Here are the default criteria in order of application:
1.  **Typos**: Sorts results based on the number of typos, placing exact matches first.
2.  **Geolocation**: Ranks results by distance from a specified location.
3.  **Words**: Prioritizes results that contain all query words over those with only some.
4.  **Filters**: Ranks based on filter matches.
5.  **Proximity**: Considers the distance between matched words in an attribute.
6.  **Attribute**: Ranks results based on the priority of the attribute that matched the query.
7.  **Exact**: A second pass that prioritizes results with more exact, typo-free matches.
8.  **Custom**: Uses your own business metrics (like popularity or rating) to rank results.

### How Tie-Breaking Works

The tie-breaking algorithm is central to how Algolia ranks results. Here is the process:
1.  Algolia first orders all matching records according to the first criterion (Typos).
2.  If any records are tied (for example, multiple records are exact matches), it then orders that group of records using the second criterion (Geolocation).
3.  This process continues down the list of criteria until each record has a unique rank [What does "This result is ranked based on objectID" mean?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean).
4.  After applying the eight main criteria, any custom ranking attributes you've defined are used to break any remaining ties.
5.  If records are still tied after all criteria have been applied, Algolia ranks them based on their internal `objectID` [What does "This result is ranked based on objectID" mean?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean).

You can see a detailed breakdown of which criteria were used to rank a specific result directly in the Algolia dashboard by hovering over the medal icon on a search result [How can I troubleshoot relevancy using Algolia dashboard?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard).

Now that you understand the default ranking, would you be interested in learning how to configure custom ranking attributes to better align the results with your business goals?

questions.prompt: How does the distinct setting deduplicate results?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How does the distinct setting deduplicate results?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

The **distinct** setting in Algolia deduplicates results by grouping records that share the same value for a specific attribute. From each group, only the single most relevant record is returned in the search results.

### How `distinct` Works

The `distinct` feature is designed to group similar items and present only the best one, which is useful for scenarios like product variants (size, color) or grouped articles.

For example, if you have an index containing multiple records for the same t-shirt, each representing a different size, the `distinct` feature can group them ([details and examples](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct-)). If a user searches for "t-shirt", instead of seeing five separate results for the same shirt, they will see only the single most relevant one.

This is achieved by setting `distinct` on an attribute that all variants share, such as a `product_id`. When a search is performed:
1.  Algolia finds all matching records.
2.  It then groups the records based on the unique values of the specified attribute.
3.  Within each group, the records are ranked according to your relevance settings.
4.  Only the top-ranking record from each group is returned as a search result.

### `distinct` and Faceting

It's important to know how `distinct` interacts with faceting. By default, facet counts are calculated *before* deduplication is applied. This can sometimes lead to facet counts that are higher than the number of hits displayed. There is a setting to calculate facet counts *after* deduplication has been applied to ensure consistency ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct-)).

In some cases, such as with the Algolia Shopify app, when results are deduplicated, the facets will only display the values from the single returned variant, not from all the variants that were grouped together ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results-)).

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
[Shopify] Can I display all variant facet values while deduplicating search results?
When you enable “Show products instead of variants” in the Algolia Shopify app, it activates the distinct feature on the id attribute on your Algolia index.The id  attribute represents the “product Id” attribute in your Shopify store, so the distinct feature will use this attribute to limit the number of returned records that contain the same value in that attribute. If your 'Product A' has 5 different sizes, your search will return only the single most relevant record; the other variants that share the same id ("product Id") will not be displayed. Facets will return the facet values that appear in the search results, so if the search results are deduplicated by the distinct parameter, your facets will only display the facet values of the returned variant. While this can be accomplished in non-Shopify implementations of Algolia by setting the facet filter as a non-afterDistinct faceting attribute (as described in this documentation), there is currently no custom hook to modify the default distinct behavior as it relates to faceting in the Algolia Shopify app.  Although we do not have an ETA at this time, our product team have added this functionality to the roadmap for a future release.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results

sources.text:
Why don't my facet counts match the number of hits for attributes set to 'after distinct'?
You may notice that when reviewing your index in the Algolia Dashboard, or on your InstantSearch frontend, that the number of hits returned by a query don't tally with the counts next to some of your facets.If this is the case, check whether the facet showing the incorrect count is set to 'after distinct' as an attribute for faceting.Setting 'after distinct' on an attribute for faceting means that facet counts will be calculated after distinct has been applied. Distinct is a method used to Group Results and is often employed in cases where you want to combine records into a single result, rather than displaying each variant as a separate hit. For example, you might have 5 records for the same t-shirt, one for each size the t-shirt is available in. The issue occurs when combining this with disjunctive faceting. Disjunctive faceting allows for the selection of multiple values within the same facet and to filter on them independently (e.g. size is Medium OR Large). It's the opposite of conjunctive faceting, where the values are treated as dependent (e.g. Medium AND Large).To allow for disjunctive faceting, InstantSearch makes two requests; one to retrieve the hits and one to retrieve the facets. The request to retrieve the facets, counts the deduplicated hits before they have been filtered, and this is what can lead to inconsistencies between the hits and the facet counts.This is a limitation of InstantSearch and the only way to ensure consistent counts under these circumstances is to use conjunctive faceting instead. This can be enabled within your InstantSearch refinementList widget by changing the operator parameter value to 'AND'. There's no way to do this within the Algolia Dashboard. There are several scenarios that could lead to incorrect or inconsistent facet counts. If this article doesn't relate to your issue, please have a look at the below article that describes other issues related to this topic: Facet and hit count issues checklist
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct

answer.answer:
The `distinct` feature deduplicates results by grouping records that share the same value for a specific attribute, and then returns only the single most relevant record from each group [[Shopify] Can I display all variant facet values while deduplicating search results?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results).

### How it works

The `distinct` feature is often used when you want to combine multiple records, like product variants, into a single result rather than showing each one separately [[Why don't my facet counts match the number of hits for attributes set to 'after distinct'?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct-?)].

For example, if you sell a t-shirt that has five different sizes, you might have five separate records in your index. With `distinct` enabled, a search will return only the most relevant of those five records, hiding the other variants that share the same product ID [[Shopify] Can I display all variant facet values while deduplicating search results?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results). The attribute used for this grouping (like a `product_id`) is what determines which records are considered duplicates.

Are you looking to implement this for a product catalog with variants, or do you have a different use case in mind?

When you enable “Show products instead of variants” in the Algolia Shopify app, it activates the distinct feature on the id attribute on your Algolia index.The id  attribute represents the “product Id” attribute in your Shopify store, so the distinct feature will use this attribute to limit the number of returned records that contain the same value in that attribute. If your 'Product A' has 5 different sizes, your search will return only the single most relevant record; the other variants that share the same id ("product Id") will not be displayed. Facets will return the facet values that appear in the search results, so if the search results are deduplicated by the distinct parameter, your facets will only display the facet values of the returned variant. While this can be accomplished in non-Shopify implementations of Algolia by setting the facet filter as a non-afterDistinct faceting attribute (as described in this documentation), there is currently no custom hook to modify the default distinct behavior as it relates to faceting in the Algolia Shopify app.  Although we do not have an ETA at this time, our product team have added this functionality to the roadmap for a future release.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results

sources.text:
Why don't my facet counts match the number of hits for attributes set to 'after distinct'?
You may notice that when reviewing your index in the Algolia Dashboard, or on your InstantSearch frontend, that the number of hits returned by a query don't tally with the counts next to some of your facets.If this is the case, check whether the facet showing the incorrect count is set to 'after distinct' as an attribute for faceting.Setting 'after distinct' on an attribute for faceting means that facet counts will be calculated after distinct has been applied. Distinct is a method used to Group Results and is often employed in cases where you want to combine records into a single result, rather than displaying each variant as a separate hit. For example, you might have 5 records for the same t-shirt, one for each size the t-shirt is available in. The issue occurs when combining this with disjunctive faceting. Disjunctive faceting allows for the selection of multiple values within the same facet and to filter on them independently (e.g. size is Medium OR Large). It's the opposite of conjunctive faceting, where the values are treated as dependent (e.g. Medium AND Large).To allow for disjunctive faceting, InstantSearch makes two requests; one to retrieve the hits and one to retrieve the facets. The request to retrieve the facets, counts the deduplicated hits before they have been filtered, and this is what can lead to inconsistencies between the hits and the facet counts.This is a limitation of InstantSearch and the only way to ensure consistent counts under these circumstances is to use conjunctive faceting instead. This can be enabled within your InstantSearch refinementList widget by changing the operator parameter value to 'AND'. There's no way to do this within the Algolia Dashboard. There are several scenarios that could lead to incorrect or inconsistent facet counts. If this article doesn't relate to your issue, please have a look at the below article that describes other issues related to this topic: Facet and hit count issues checklist
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct

questions.prompt: How do I configure searchable attributes the right way?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How do I configure searchable attributes the right way?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

To configure searchable attributes correctly, you must explicitly define a prioritized list of the attributes in your records that you want the Algolia engine to search through. The order of these attributes is crucial as it directly impacts search relevance ([details and examples](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/searchable-attributes/how-to/configuring-searchable-attributes-the-right-way/)).

### What are Searchable Attributes?
**Searchable attributes** are the fields in your dataset that Algolia's engine scans for matching query words. By default, Algolia makes all attributes searchable. However, if your records have different structures, this can lead to inconsistent search behavior. Explicitly defining your searchable attributes ensures that search is predictable and relevant ([Why isn't Algolia searching all my records' attributes?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes-)).

### Ordered vs. Unordered Attributes
You can classify attributes as either **ordered** or **unordered**:
*   **Ordered**: The position of the matched word within the attribute matters. This is ideal for structured, important text like a product title or brand name, where a match at the beginning is more relevant.
*   **Unordered**: The position of the matched word doesn't matter. This is suitable for less structured text like a product description or a blog post's content.

### How Order Affects Ranking
The order in which you list your searchable attributes is a key part of Algolia's **ranking formula**. The engine uses this order to decide which of two matching records should rank higher. A match in an attribute that is higher on your list (e.g., `title`) will be considered more relevant than a match in an attribute lower on the list (e.g., `description`).

### Best Practices for Configuration
Here is a recommended approach for ordering your searchable attributes for an ecommerce site:

1.  **Unique Identifiers**: Start with attributes containing unique identifiers like `SKU` or `objectID`. This ensures that exact matches for product codes always appear first.
2.  **Brand and Categories**: Place attributes like `brand` and `categories` next. These are highly specific and important to users.
3.  **Titles and Names**: The `title` or `name` of the item should follow. These are often the most important searchable fields.
4.  **Less Structured Text**: Place attributes with longer, less structured text like `description` at the end of the list.

By following this prioritization, you ensure that the most relevant results are returned to the user first ([Configure searchable attributes the right way](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/searchable-attributes/how-to/configuring-searchable-attributes-the-right-way/)).

For more in-depth training, you can also refer to the [Algolia Academy](https://academy.algolia.com/training/019465d6-0539-7661-8640-9b0c3656c165).

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Why isn't Algolia searching all my records' attributes?
If Algolia doesn’t appear to be searching across every attribute in your records, the issue may be related to your Searchable Attributes configuration. This article explains how Algolia determines which attributes are searchable, and why explicitly configuring them can help ensure consistent behavior.Default behavior: all attributes are searchableBy default, when you create an index in Algolia and don't configure Searchable Attributes, every attribute in every record is searchable. This means you can start using search right away without any manual setup.So why might it not work as expected?If your records don’t all share the same schema, for example, if some objects are missing certain fields, leaving Searchable Attributes empty can lead to inconsistent search behavior. Algolia may not reliably search an attribute that isn't present in every record, making it seem like some attributes are being ignored.Recommended solution: define your searchable attributesTo avoid unpredictability, especially when your data structure varies between records, we strongly recommend configuring the Searchable Attributes explicitly in the Configuration tab of the Dashboard (or via the API). This lets you:
Control which fields are searchable
Prioritize more important attributes
Ensure consistent behavior across all queries
Summary
All attributes are searchable by default, but only when all records share the same schema.
If your records vary, leaving Searchable Attributes empty can cause unexpected results.
To ensure consistent and predictable search behavior, explicitly define your Searchable Attributes.

answer.answer:
Configuring searchable attributes correctly is one of the most important steps to ensure relevant search results. You control which record attributes the engine searches by explicitly defining them in the `searchableAttributes` list for your index.

While Algolia makes all attributes searchable by default, this can lead to inconsistent behavior if your records don't all have the same structure [Why isn't Algolia searching all my records' attributes?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes). For this reason, it's a best practice to explicitly define this list to control which fields are searchable and to prioritize them.

### How to configure attributes

The "right way" to configure your searchable attributes involves creating a prioritized list that tells the engine which attributes are most important [Configure searchable attributes the right way](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/searchable-attributes/how-to/configuring-searchable-attributes-the-right-way/). You can do this from your Algolia Dashboard in the **Configuration** tab for your index, or programmatically via the API.

There are two ways to list an attribute:

*   **Ordered (default)**: The order of attributes in the list directly impacts relevance. An attribute listed earlier is considered more important. A match found in the first attribute in your list will rank higher than a match found in the second, and so on. You should list them in decreasing order of importance.
    *   *Example*: For an ecommerce site, you would want to list `product_name` before `description`, because a match in the name is a much stronger signal of relevance.

*   **Unordered**: For attributes where a match is relevant but doesn't imply more or less importance than another, you can use the `unordered` modifier. This is useful for attributes like `tags`, `categories`, or `SKU`. A match in any unordered attribute is treated equally from a ranking perspective.

A typical configuration might look like this:
`['product_name', 'brand', 'categories', 'description', 'unordered(SKU)']`

By setting this list, you ensure consistent and predictable search behavior, which is a key step in building a powerful search experience [How to get started with Algolia?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406821689361-How-to-get-started-with-Algolia). You can learn more about how to optimize these settings in the [Configuring Searchable Attributes](https://academy.algolia.com/training/019465d6-0539-7661-8640-9b0c3656c165) course.

What kind of data are you indexing? Knowing that can help you decide on the best priority order for your attributes.

questions.prompt: How do I implement backend search with React InstantSearch?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How do I implement backend search with React InstantSearch?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

To implement backend search with React InstantSearch, you create a custom search client that sends search requests to your own backend instead of directly to Algolia. Your backend then uses an Algolia API client to perform the search and returns the results to the frontend.

### Backend Search with React InstantSearch

With **backend search**, your frontend, built with **React InstantSearch**, doesn't communicate directly with Algolia. Instead, it sends search requests to your own server. Your server, which securely holds your Algolia API key, then makes the actual search request to Algolia and passes the results back to the frontend.

This approach is useful when you need to preprocess search parameters or enrich the search results with data from other sources before sending them to the user ([details and examples](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/backend-search/react/)).

### Implementation Steps

1.  **Set up your backend:**
    *   Your server needs to have an endpoint (e.g., `/api/search`) that receives search requests from your React InstantSearch frontend.
    *   This endpoint uses an official Algolia **API client** to perform the search. This is where your secret Algolia API key is used.

2.  **Create a custom search client on the frontend:**
    *   In your React application, you need to build a custom **search client**.
    *   This client's role is to call your backend endpoint, passing along the search parameters.
    *   It should handle sending the requests and receiving the JSON response from your server.

3.  **Use the custom client in your InstantSearch component:**
    *   Finally, you provide your custom search client to the `<InstantSearch>` component. React InstantSearch will then use your backend for all search operations.

For a detailed guide on how to implement this, including code examples for the custom search client and the server-side endpoint, please refer to the official documentation on [implementing backend search with React InstantSearch](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/backend-search/in-depth/backend-instantsearch/react/).

For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Senior Full-Stack Software Engineer, Frontend Experiences

At Algolia, we’re proud to be a pioneer and market leader in AI Search, empowering 17,000+ businesses to deliver blazing-fast, predictive search and browse experiences at internet scale. Every week, we power over 30 billion search requests — four times more than Microsoft Bing, Yahoo, Baidu, Yandex, and DuckDuckGo combined.

In 2021, we raised $150 million in Series D funding, quadrupling our valuation to $2.25 billion. This strong foundation enables us to keep investing in our market-leading platform and serving incredible customers like Under Armour, PetSmart, Stripe, Gymshark, and Walgreens.

The Team

The Frontend Experiences (FX) team is responsible for inspiring and empowering Algolia users to build the best Algolia powered UI experiences for their needs. We imagine, create and decompose elaborated UI experiences into small blocks ready to be mixed and matched in our users’ apps. We maintain a set of UI libraries such as Autocomplete, which you may have come across while searching the Tailwind or React docs. We also maintain the InstantSearch library platform which unlocks complex and modern Search & Discovery use cases.

We don’t use a single framework, we use them all. Our libraries work on top of Vue.js, React but also plain JavaScript (“Vanilla JS”). We write specs for every feature we release. Our code is in TypeScript, fully open source and tested. 

We don’t only do frontend, we also maintain certain backend features that support our experiences, in TypeScript, Go and Python.

The Opportunity

We're looking for a Fullstack Engineer to join the Frontend Experiences (FX) team at Algolia. As a member, you will have a direct impact on the look and feel of Algolia powered UIs. You’ll help customers re-imagine the future of how people search and discover information. You will play a pivotal role in architecting our platform, which is the face of Algolia’s Developer Experience.

Your role will consist of: 

  • Architect, design and develop new versions of InstantSearch platform, including our open source libraries and our backend APIs.
  • Design and implement features that leverage agentic patterns within the Algolia platform and our open-source libraries.
  • Implement new features, solve issues and analyse user's feedback on our popular open-source projects
  • Coordinate with our product, design and marketing teams on the release of new projects and websites
  • Take ownership, research, explore and deliver novel experiences
  • Teach us what we don't know and how you want to improve the search and discovery experiences
  • Participate (new features, bug fixes) to other popular open-source projects related to our project needs
  • Be a technical mentor and help the team grow
  • Help define engineering best practices and processes

You might be a fit if you have:

  • Demonstrable proficiency with the latest agentic experience design, development patterns, and relevant SDKs.
  • Deep understanding of building, integrating, and optimizing user interfaces leveraging autonomous agents, LLMs, and AI-driven workflows.
  • Good knowledge of JavaScript, TypeScript, the Frontend web ecosystem: DOM, frameworks, library crafting, bundlers
  • You have created at least one reusable module (private or open source)
  • You have an eye for great user experiences (UX)
  • You like to help, teach and mentor your colleagues, in and outside the team.
  • Experience at our current stage and beyond ($50-300M ARR range, high growth, lots of change and building internal infrastructure).

Overall, we care about your current and future skill set. Not where you studied or who you know. We care about what you like to do and about what you'd like to do in the future, at Algolia.



#LI-Remote

FLEXIBLE WORKPLACE STRATEGY:

Algolia’s flexible workplace model is designed to empower all Algolians to fulfill our mission to power search and discovery with ease. We place an emphasis on an individual’s impact, contribution, and output, over their physical location. Algolia is a high-trust environment and many of our team members have the autonomy to choose where they want to work and when. 

We have a global presence with offices in Paris, NYC, London, Sydney and Bucharest, however we also offer many of our team members the option to work remotely either as fully remote or hybrid-remote employees. Positions listed as "Remote" are only available for remote work within the specified country. Positions listed within a specific city are only available in that location - depending on the role it may be available with either a hybrid-remote or in-office schedule.

WE’RE LOOKING FOR SOMEONE WHO CAN LIVE OUR VALUES:

  • GRIT - Problem-solving and perseverance capability in an ever-changing and growing environment.
  • TRUST - Willingness to trust our co-workers and to take ownership.
  • CANDOR - Ability to receive and give constructive feedback.
  • CARE - Genuine care about other team members, our clients and the decisions we make in the company.
  • HUMILITY - Aptitude for learning from others, putting ego aside.

We’re looking for talented, passionate people to help build the world’s best search and discovery technology. We value autonomy, diversity, and collaboration. We’re committed to creating an inclusive workplace where everyone is respected and supported—regardless of race, age, ancestry, religion, sex, gender identity, sexual orientation, marital status, color, veteran status, disability, or socioeconomic background.

IMPORTANT NOTICE FOR CANDIDATES - Recruitment Fraud Notice

We’ve recently seen an increase in recruitment scams targeting job seekers. To help protect yourself, please keep the following in mind:

  • Our open positions may appear on third-party job boards, but the best way to apply safely is directly through our careers page.
  • All genuine communication from Algolia will come from an @algolia.com email address. If you receive an email from someone claiming to work at Algolia who does not have an @algolia.com email address, please do not respond or share any personal information.
  • We’ll never ask for payments, purchases, or financial details during the hiring process.

READY TO APPLY?

If you share our values and our enthusiasm for building the world’s best search & discovery technology, we’d love to review your application!

answer.answer:
To implement backend search with React InstantSearch, you use the `findResultsState` function to enable server-side rendering (SSR). This approach is primarily for improving SEO by serving fully rendered HTML to search engine crawlers and for providing a faster initial experience for users, especially those with JavaScript disabled [Backend search with React InstantSearch](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/server-side-rendering/react/).

### How it works

Server-side rendering with React InstantSearch involves two main stages:

1.  **On the server:** You fetch the initial search results from Algolia. The `findResultsState` function from `react-instantsearch-dom/server` helps you retrieve the search state corresponding to the initial UI state of your application.
2.  **On the client:** You pass the server-fetched results to the `<InstantSearch>` component. The component then uses these initial results to render the page without needing to make another API call on the initial load.

This process ensures that the first thing the user or a web crawler sees is a complete page with search results already populated [Server-side rendering with React InstantSearch](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/server-side-rendering/react/).

### Implementation steps

Here is a summary of the steps to set up backend search:

1.  **Install dependencies**: You will need `react-instantsearch-dom`.
2.  **Create your server**: In your `server.js` file, you use the `findResultsState` function on your React InstantSearch app. This function simulates a render on the server to determine what search results are needed.
3.  **Pass initial results**: The state retrieved from `findResultsState` is then passed as a prop (e.g., `initialResults`) to your client-side application.
4.  **Initialize on the client**: In your main app component, you pass the `initialResults` prop to the `<InstantSearch>` widget. This hydrates the component with the data fetched from the server.
5.  **Configure routing**: Use the `routing` prop on the `<InstantSearch>` component to synchronize the search state with the URL. This is crucial for SSR, as it ensures that URLs can be bookmarked, shared, and correctly indexed by crawlers [Backend search with React InstantSearch](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/server-side-rendering/react/).

You can get started quickly by using a template like `create-instantsearch-app` and selecting the server-side rendering option [Backend search with React InstantSearch](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/server-side-rendering/react/).

Are you implementing this for a new application, or are you adding it to an existing one?

questions.prompt: What is NeuralSearch and how does it rank results?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "What is NeuralSearch and how does it rank results?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

Algolia NeuralSearch is an AI-powered search technology that provides highly relevant results by understanding the concepts and intent behind a user's query, not just matching keywords. It combines keyword-based search with semantic understanding to handle natural language and complex questions.

### How NeuralSearch Works

**NeuralSearch** enhances traditional keyword search by using large language models (LLMs) to grasp the meaning and context of a query. This allows it to find conceptually related results, even if they don't contain the exact keywords used in the search bar ([details](https://academy.algolia.com/training/01995974-63ae-715c-b8cc-b91ded19ae3d)). It is designed to understand customer intent, leading to more relevant and accurate results ([details](/products/features/neuralsearch)).

### Ranking and Relevance

Instead of relying solely on text-based relevance, NeuralSearch adds a layer of **semantic understanding** to its ranking. Here’s how it improves result ranking:

*   **Conceptual Matching**: It finds products or content that are conceptually related to the search query. For example, a search for "something to keep my hands warm" could return results for "gloves" or "mittens" even if the exact words aren't in the product data.
*   **User Intent**: By analyzing the language of a query, it deciphers what the user is truly looking for and ranks the most relevant items higher.
*   **Explainability**: Algolia provides tools to understand the "ranking factors" behind your NeuralSearch results. This allows you to see why certain results are ranked higher than others and provides insights into how the AI is interpreting queries ([details](https://academy.algolia.com/training/7ea73420-0247-11ef-90fa-02a84b0442e5)).

You can also test, configure, and fine-tune the behavior of NeuralSearch to ensure it aligns with your specific business goals before deploying it live ([details](https://academy.algolia.com/training/019959a9-cbb5-7003-b8e2-e2856b40f930)).

What happens if we set analytics as false for a query but set clickAnalytics as true? Will click analytics still work and set a queryID for searches so I can send events?
Yes, the analytics and click analytics search parameters are separate.If you set analytics as false (which means that the search will not appear in your analytics) you can still set clickAnalytics as true, and use the search to send events.However this click event will not be tracked in your analytics, because there will be no search in your analytics to link it back to. As there will be no search information, the events can be used for Algolia Recommend or Personalization but they will not work with NeuralSearch, Dynamic Re-Ranking, Query Categorisation or A/B Testing which all require events to be linked to a search.

Privacy manifest
Note: If your Algolia implementation collects tracking data, Apple requires you to add a privacy manifest that describes your reason and method for collecting tracking data.
 
What is a privacy manifest?
A privacy manifest is a file in your Xcode project that describes the reason your app and third-party SDKs collect data, along with their data-collection methods. Each of your third-party SDKs that track data require their own privacy manifest. When you create your app’s privacy report, these privacy manifest files are automatically aggregated into a single report.
 
Declaring your privacy manifest
Step 1: Review current policies
Before you begin, review your Algolia SDK’s current data collection policies and API usage within your application. Determine which elements of your application collect data and what APIs you are using.
 
See the following pages for more documentation about the collection of end-user data by Algolia’s SDK’s. Please note that most of the time, end-user data collection is needed for Algolia features to operate (e.g. Recommend, Re-Ranking, Personalisation, Neuralsearch).

User token
Event types
Get started with click and conversion events

 
Step 2: Declare data collection practices
We have provided template privacy manifests for both the InstantSearch iOS Library and the Swift API Client. You can start with these templates to ensure you cover all required aspects.
By default, the Algolia SDK does not collect tracking data. However, depending on your Algolia SDK configuration, you may be required to list Algolia-specific data in your app’s privacy manifest.
 
Step 3: Customize privacy manifest
Edit the provided files to reflect the specific data collection and API usage of your applications. Ensure each entry is clearly justified and aligns with Apple’s guidelines.
 
Step 4: Integrate privacy manifest into application
Once your privacy manifest is customized and complete, integrate into your iOS project. This involves adding your files to your project settings and ensuring it is included in your build process.

answer.answer:
Algolia NeuralSearch is a feature that uses AI to understand the meaning and concepts in a search query, not just the keywords. It then re-ranks the results from Algolia's keyword search to find the best semantic match between the query and the content of your records [Neural Search 101](https://www.algolia.com/blog/ai/what-is-neural-search-and-how-does-it-work/). This approach is particularly effective for long, natural language queries and unstructured content like articles or product descriptions [Get started with NeuralSearch](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/ai-relevance/neuralsearch/get-started/).

### How NeuralSearch ranks results

NeuralSearch works as a re-ranking model on top of Algolia's core keyword search engine. The process happens in two main steps:

1.  **Keyword Search**: First, Algolia's standard search engine retrieves all results that match the keywords in the query.
2.  **AI Re-ranking**: Next, NeuralSearch takes the top results from the keyword search and re-ranks them based on their semantic relevance to the query. It uses large language models (LLMs) to compare the meaning of the user's query with the meaning of the text in your records.

This means NeuralSearch doesn't retrieve new results; it reorders the most relevant results from the initial keyword search to better match the user's intent [Get started with NeuralSearch](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/ai-relevance/neuralsearch/get-started/). It acts as the final step in the ranking process, applied after textual relevance and any custom ranking you have configured.

To use this feature, you'll need a plan that includes the AI Search add-on [Get started with NeuralSearch](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/ai-relevance/neuralsearch/get-started/).

What kind of content or user queries are you hoping to improve with a feature like NeuralSearch?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "What is vector search?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

Vector search is a method used to find items that are semantically similar to a user's query, rather than relying on exact keyword matches. It works by representing data—such as text, images, or audio—as numerical vectors in a high-dimensional space.

### How Vector Search Works

Vector search uses AI models to convert unstructured data into numerical representations called **vector embeddings**. These embeddings capture the meaning and context of the original data.

1.  **Embedding Creation**: An AI model, like a neural network, processes data (e.g., product descriptions, articles, images) and converts each item into a vector. These vectors are then stored in a specialized database.
2.  **Querying**: When a user enters a query, it is also converted into a vector using the same AI model.
3.  **Finding Similarity**: The system then searches the database for vectors that are "closest" to the query vector. The proximity between vectors indicates semantic similarity ([details and examples](https://www.algolia.com/blog/ai/what-is-vector-search)).

### Key Concepts

*   **Semantic Search**: This is the core capability powered by vector search. It allows the search engine to understand the intent and contextual meaning behind a query. For example, a search for "clothes for hot weather" could return results like "shorts," "linen shirts," and "sun dresses," even if the exact keywords aren't in the product descriptions.
*   **Vector Embeddings**: These are the numerical representations of data. They are the cornerstone of vector search, as they translate complex data into a format that a machine can use to understand relationships and similarity.

By moving beyond keywords to understand concepts and context, vector search provides more relevant and intuitive results, especially for complex or abstract queries ([read more on the evolution of search](https://www.algolia.com/resources/asset/infographic-evolutionofsearch)).

For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How does Algolia support ecommerce and retail search?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

Algolia provides a powerful suite of AI-powered search, discovery, and recommendation tools that help ecommerce and retail businesses create fast, relevant, and personalized experiences to drive conversions and revenue.

### Core Search and Discovery

Algolia's platform is designed to handle the specific needs of ecommerce and retail, focusing on speed, relevance, and user experience.

*   **Blazing-Fast Search:** Algolia delivers search results in milliseconds, providing a seamless and responsive experience for shoppers. This speed is crucial for keeping users engaged and reducing bounce rates.
*   **Advanced Relevance:** The platform offers highly tunable relevance, allowing businesses to control how search results are ranked based on business metrics like popularity, stock levels, or promotional status.
*   **Predictive Experiences:** Algolia helps power predictive search and browse experiences at scale, anticipating user needs to help them find what they're looking for faster.

### AI-Powered Ecommerce Features

Algolia leverages AI and machine learning to enhance the shopping experience and improve business outcomes.

*   **Semantic Search:** Algolia utilizes advanced AI models, including Large Language Models (LLMs), to understand the intent behind a user's query. This allows for more natural and intuitive search, moving beyond simple keyword matching to understand context and meaning, which is crucial for real-world retail applications ([details and examples](https://www.algolia.com/resources/asset/ebook-reevaluating-llm-encoders)).
*   **AI Agents and Future of Commerce:** The platform is continuously evolving with features like agentic AI, which is set to transform retail technology by creating more interactive and intelligent shopping assistants ([read more on retail tech trends](https://www.algolia.com/blog/ecommerce/retail-tech-trends-agentic-ai-and-the-future-of-commerce)).
*   **Personalization:** Algolia enables one-to-one personalization, tailoring the search and discovery experience to each user's individual behavior and preferences, which helps increase engagement and conversion rates.

By combining a robust search foundation with cutting-edge AI capabilities, Algolia empowers over 17,000 businesses, including major retailers like Under Armour, PetSmart, and Walgreens, to build modern, high-converting digital commerce experiences.

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Commercial Account Executive, DACH

At Algolia, we’re proud to be a pioneer and market leader in AI Search, empowering 17,000+ businesses to deliver blazing-fast, predictive search and browse experiences at internet scale. Every week, we power over 30 billion search requests — four times more than Microsoft Bing, Yahoo, Baidu, Yandex, and DuckDuckGo combined.

In 2021, we raised $150 million in Series D funding, quadrupling our valuation to $2.25 billion. This strong foundation enables us to keep investing in our market-leading platform and serving incredible customers like Under Armour, PetSmart, Stripe, Gymshark, and Walgreens.

At Algolia, we are passionate about helping businesses connect their end-users with what matters most in milliseconds!

 

As an Algolia Account Executive, you will be responsible for creating strong relationships with our customers/prospects and building on these relationships to expand our footprint or close new businesses. You should be comfortable researching prospective customers and adapting sales strategies to meet their needs. You will also play an integral role in defining our sales model. You know the ins and outs of Algolia, understand how we can benefit each prospect and will ultimately be the front line of our revenue growth in the German-speaking and CEMEA markets.

 

Algolia has been growing exponentially over the past few years with Germany representing one of our core markets. We are now expanding our existing German-speaking team to further accelerate in this region, providing you with the opportunity to become one of the core players in our success story. You will be an important voice in how we address this market and able to gain a lot of visibility both within our fast-growing tech company as well as within the German eCommerce/ retail segment.

 

Are you a resilient problem-solver who isn't afraid to think outside the box and get their hands dirty? We expect you to take ownership and be able to execute, defining your own territory strategy. We're looking for candidates who raise the level and bring sales excellence to the revenue organisation. You should value and practice transparency, have the humility to accept your weaknesses and continuously strive to improve both personally and professionally.

YOUR ROLE WILL CONSIST OF:

  • Managing and growing a sales pipeline
  • Driving inbound and outbound leads through complex sales cycles to closed won
  • Working your network and collaborating with external partners to source and facilitate new projects in the Commercial segment
  • Managing and prospecting with an existing account base to drive usage-based and feature-based upsells
  • Generating new projects from booking self-driven outbound meetings
  • Building compelling demonstrations with the pre-sales team, sales material and ROI studies bringing value to our customers
  • Forecasting accurately to help the team hit their numbers
  • Collaborating with sales developers, solutions engineers, customer success managers, legal, partner managers and marketing.

YOU MIGHT BE A FIT IF YOU HAVE:

  • 2 - 4 years of experience in a closing role (esp. in B2B, MarTech, SaaS tech sales)
  • Excellent spoken and written German and English skills, Polish or Czech a plus
  • Hunter mindset: Strong familiarity with outbound prospecting techniques, tools and processes
  • Strong Plus: Experience in working with partners (solution integrators, consultancies, independent software vendors)
  • Multi-tasker who can adapt to changing situations
  • Quick thinker, must be able to handle questions on the fly

NICE TO HAVE:

  • You love tech sales;
  • You have a curious mindset and are capable to understand prospects’ pains and how a deep tech solution like Algolia can solve it;
  • You thrive in dynamic environments and are capable to adjust to constant change;
  • You are excited by the idea of building things and have a structured and strategic approach to working your territory;
  • You believe you are in charge of your own success and you have the grit to continue going when things get challenging.

#LI-Hybrid 

FLEXIBLE WORKPLACE STRATEGY:

Algolia’s flexible workplace model is designed to empower all Algolians to fulfill our mission to power search and discovery with ease. We place an emphasis on an individual’s impact, contribution, and output, over their physical location. Algolia is a high-trust environment and many of our team members have the autonomy to choose where they want to work and when. 

We have a global presence with offices in Paris, NYC, London, Sydney and Bucharest, however we also offer many of our team members the option to work remotely either as fully remote or hybrid-remote employees. Positions listed as "Remote" are only available for remote work within the specified country. Positions listed within a specific city are only available in that location - depending on the role it may be available with either a hybrid-remote or in-office schedule.

WE’RE LOOKING FOR SOMEONE WHO CAN LIVE OUR VALUES:

  • GRIT - Problem-solving and perseverance capability in an ever-changing and growing environment.
  • TRUST - Willingness to trust our co-workers and to take ownership.
  • CANDOR - Ability to receive and give constructive feedback.
  • CARE - Genuine care about other team members, our clients and the decisions we make in the company.
  • HUMILITY - Aptitude for learning from others, putting ego aside.

We’re looking for talented, passionate people to help build the world’s best search and discovery technology. We value autonomy, diversity, and collaboration. We’re committed to creating an inclusive workplace where everyone is respected and supported—regardless of race, age, ancestry, religion, sex, gender identity, sexual orientation, marital status, color, veteran status, disability, or socioeconomic background.

IMPORTANT NOTICE FOR CANDIDATES - Recruitment Fraud Notice

We’ve recently seen an increase in recruitment scams targeting job seekers. To help protect yourself, please keep the following in mind:

  • Our open positions may appear on third-party job boards, but the best way to apply safely is directly through our careers page.
  • All genuine communication from Algolia will come from an @algolia.com email address. If you receive an email from someone claiming to work at Algolia who does not have an @algolia.com email address, please do not respond or share any personal information.
  • We’ll never ask for payments, purchases, or financial details during the hiring process.

READY TO APPLY?

If you share our values and our enthusiasm for building the world’s best search & discovery technology, we’d love to review your application!

In 2021, we raised $150 million in Series D funding, quadrupling our valuation to $2.25 billion. This strong foundation enables us to keep investing in our market-leading platform and serving incredible customers like Under Armour, PetSmart, Stripe, Gymshark, and Walgreens.

At Algolia, we are passionate about helping businesses connect their end-users with what matters most in milliseconds!

 

As an Algolia Account Executive, you will be responsible for creating strong relationships with our customers/prospects and building on these relationships to expand our footprint or close new businesses. You should be comfortable researching prospective customers and adapting sales strategies to meet their needs. You will also play an integral role in defining our sales model. You know the ins and outs of Algolia, understand how we can benefit each prospect and will ultimately be the front line of our revenue growth in the German-speaking and CEMEA markets.

 

Algolia has been growing exponentially over the past few years with Germany representing one of our core markets. We are now expanding our existing German-speaking team to further accelerate in this region, providing you with the opportunity to become one of the core players in our success story. You will be an important voice in how we address this market and able to gain a lot of visibility both within our fast-growing tech company as well as within the German eCommerce/ retail segment.

 

Are you a resilient problem-solver who isn't afraid to think outside the box and get their hands dirty? We expect you to take ownership and be able to execute, defining your own territory strategy. We're looking for candidates who raise the level and bring sales excellence to the revenue organisation. You should value and practice transparency, have the humility to accept your weaknesses and continuously strive to improve both personally and professionally.

YOUR ROLE WILL CONSIST OF:

  • Managing and growing a sales pipeline
  • Driving inbound and outbound leads through complex sales cycles to closed won
  • Working your network and collaborating with external partners to source and facilitate new projects in the Commercial segment
  • Managing and prospecting with an existing account base to drive usage-based and feature-based upsells
  • Generating new projects from booking self-driven outbound meetings
  • Building compelling demonstrations with the pre-sales team, sales material and ROI studies bringing value to our customers
  • Forecasting accurately to help the team hit their numbers
  • Collaborating with sales developers, solutions engineers, customer success managers, legal, partner managers and marketing.

YOU MIGHT BE A FIT IF YOU HAVE:

  • 2 - 4 years of experience in a closing role (esp. in B2B, MarTech, SaaS tech sales)
  • Excellent spoken and written German and English skills, Polish or Czech a plus
  • Hunter mindset: Strong familiarity with outbound prospecting techniques, tools and processes
  • Strong Plus: Experience in working with partners (solution integrators, consultancies, independent software vendors)
  • Multi-tasker who can adapt to changing situations
  • Quick thinker, must be able to handle questions on the fly

NICE TO HAVE:

  • You love tech sales;
  • You have a curious mindset and are capable to understand prospects’ pains and how a deep tech solution like Algolia can solve it;
  • You thrive in dynamic environments and are capable to adjust to constant change;
  • You are excited by the idea of building things and have a structured and strategic approach to working your territory;
  • You believe you are in charge of your own success and you have the grit to continue going when things get challenging.

#LI-Hybrid 

FLEXIBLE WORKPLACE STRATEGY:

Algolia’s flexible workplace model is designed to empower all Algolians to fulfill our mission to power search and discovery with ease. We place an emphasis on an individual’s impact, contribution, and output, over their physical location. Algolia is a high-trust environment and many of our team members have the autonomy to choose where they want to work and when. 

We have a global presence with offices in Paris, NYC, London, Sydney and Bucharest, however we also offer many of our team members the option to work remotely either as fully remote or hybrid-remote employees. Positions listed as "Remote" are only available for remote work within the specified country. Positions listed within a specific city are only available in that location - depending on the role it may be available with either a hybrid-remote or in-office schedule.

WE’RE LOOKING FOR SOMEONE WHO CAN LIVE OUR VALUES:

  • GRIT - Problem-solving and perseverance capability in an ever-changing and growing environment.
  • TRUST - Willingness to trust our co-workers and to take ownership.
  • CANDOR - Ability to receive and give constructive feedback.
  • CARE - Genuine care about other team members, our clients and the decisions we make in the company.
  • HUMILITY - Aptitude for learning from others, putting ego aside.

We’re looking for talented, passionate people to help build the world’s best search and discovery technology. We value autonomy, diversity, and collaboration. We’re committed to creating an inclusive workplace where everyone is respected and supported—regardless of race, age, ancestry, religion, sex, gender identity, sexual orientation, marital status, color, veteran status, disability, or socioeconomic background.

IMPORTANT NOTICE FOR CANDIDATES - Recruitment Fraud Notice

We’ve recently seen an increase in recruitment scams targeting job seekers. To help protect yourself, please keep the following in mind:

  • Our open positions may appear on third-party job boards, but the best way to apply safely is directly through our careers page.
  • All genuine communication from Algolia will come from an @algolia.com email address. If you receive an email from someone claiming to work at Algolia who does not have an @algolia.com email address, please do not respond or share any personal information.
  • We’ll never ask for payments, purchases, or financial details during the hiring process.

READY TO APPLY?

If you share our values and our enthusiasm for building the world’s best search & discovery technology, we’d love to review your application!

questions.prompt: How does Algolia support fashion ecommerce search?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How does Algolia support fashion ecommerce search?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

Algolia provides an **Intelligent Fashion Solution** designed to handle the unique complexities of fashion ecommerce, helping retailers create a more fluid and curated shopping experience ([details](https://www.algolia.com/about/news/algolia-launches-intelligent-fashion-solution)). This is powered by AI-driven search that goes beyond simple keywords to better understand shopper intent.

### AI-Powered Search and Discovery

For fashion and luxury retail, understanding a shopper's intent is key. Algolia's AI helps redefine the search experience to be more intuitive and effective ([read more](https://www.algolia.com/blog/ecommerce/rethinking-search-for-luxury-fashion)).

*   **Natural Language Understanding:** The search is designed to comprehend complex and subjective fashion queries, such as "boho chic" or "summer vibe," rather than just matching keywords.
*   **Curated Experiences:** AI helps to create a more curated and less transactional shopping journey, which is crucial for luxury brands that want to replicate their in-store experience online.
*   **Personalization:** The technology helps personalize the shopping experience, ensuring the results are relevant to each user's unique style and preferences.

### The Impact of AI on Fashion

AI is transforming both the art and science of fashion retail. By leveraging AI, brands can better understand and predict consumer behavior, leading to improved discovery and conversion ([learn more](https://www.algolia.com/blog/ecommerce/searchlight-series-ai-fashion-impact)). This allows fashion retailers to optimize how shoppers find and interact with their products, from initial search to final purchase.

For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

answer.answer:
Algolia helps drive conversions and improve user experience on fashion and retail websites through key site search strategies and features. For instance, features like image search are increasingly popular in the fashion and retail sectors [UI Design Kit (Figma)](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/resources/ui-kit/js).

### Key Ecommerce Search Practices

To help shoppers find products faster, it's crucial to implement a variety of search best practices. These strategies are designed to increase conversions and enhance the overall user experience. According to the [Ecommerce Site Search: Best Practices, Examples, + Tools](https://www.algolia.com/blog/ecommerce/15-best-practices-for-ecommerce-on-site-search) guide, focusing on a well-designed and intuitive search is key. While the guide covers many aspects of ecommerce, for fashion this often includes:

*   **Faceted Search:** Allowing shoppers to refine results by attributes like size, color, brand, and style.
*   **Typo Tolerance:** Ensuring that small spelling mistakes don't lead to zero results.
*   **Synonym Management:** Treating terms like "handbag" and "purse" or "sneakers" and "trainers" as equivalent to show the most relevant items.
*   **Dynamic Filtering:** Updating results in real-time as users select and deselect filters.

### Mobile Experience

A seamless mobile experience is critical for fashion ecommerce. One of the most important features is a well-performing autocomplete, also known as autosuggest. This helps users find what they are looking for quickly without having to type their full query, which is especially useful on smaller screens [3 best practices for search autocomplete on mobile](https://www.algolia.com/fr/blog/ecommerce/search-autocomplete-on-mobile).

Are you currently focused on improving a specific part of your user experience, such as your filtering options or the mobile search bar?
